scenario: Conditional 2-week US-Iran ceasefire announced Apr 8; Brent -18% to ~$97/bbl; Iran claims Hormuz still closed (Israeli Lebanon strikes); Vance-Iran talks in Pakistan Apr 12

categories.label: Food & Agriculture
categories.supplyChainImpact: Middle East urea and DAP fertilizer exports transit Hormuz, supplying ~30% of Asia's planted-area inputs. Shipping delays of 10-14 days via Cape route raise input costs and threaten planting windows for Vietnam, Thailand, and Indonesia rice belts. Palm oil - Asia's dominant cooking oil - faces surging logistics costs as Malaysia and Indonesia divert vessels.

assets.name: Wheat
assets.narrative: Ceasefire relief reduces the tail risk of a prolonged Gulf fertilizer disruption. Wheat sold off as oil and energy input costs fell on the ceasefire. Gulf urea dislocation persists while Hormuz is claimed closed by Iran, keeping fertilizer stress above pre-crisis levels. Source: Barchart ZWK26.

assets.name: Corn
assets.narrative: Biofuel demand correlation eases as Brent crashes ~18% on ceasefire. Indonesian ethanol demand adjusts lower with oil. Fertilizer and freight costs remain elevated while Hormuz is claimed closed, but the planting-season cost shock has partially unwound.

assets.name: Soybeans
assets.narrative: Bunker cost relief as Brent falls ~18% reduces freight-driven soy pressure. Indonesian and Vietnamese crush margins improve modestly. Iran's continued Hormuz claim means Gulf supply is not yet normalized, keeping spreads above pre-crisis levels.

assets.name: Palm Oil (CPO)
assets.narrative: CPO sold off sharply as Brent crude correlation reverses on the ceasefire. Biodiesel feedstock premium compresses with oil at ~$97/bbl. Indonesia's B40 mandate remains intact. Fertilizer disruption risk for next yield cycle is partially unwound but not resolved. Source: Bursa Malaysia BMD.

assets.name: Rice (Thai 25% FOB)
assets.narrative: ASEAN food security pressure eases modestly on ceasefire: freight and energy input costs fall with oil. Fertilizer shortfall remains while Hormuz is claimed closed. Philippines and Thailand bilateral routing helps but structural fertilizer availability remains constrained through Q2 planting season.

categories.label: Petrochemicals & Plastics
categories.supplyChainImpact: SABIC (Saudi), QAPCO (Qatar), and OCI (Oman) are among the world's largest petrochemical exporters - all ship via Hormuz. SE Asian crackers in Singapore, Thailand, and Malaysia rely on naphtha feedstock sourced from Gulf condensate. Downstream plastics, packaging, and agrochemicals face double squeeze: higher feedstock costs and logistics disruption.

assets.name: Naphtha (CFR Japan)
assets.narrative: Naphtha reprices sharply lower tracking Brent's ~18% ceasefire crash. However, Gulf condensate supply is still not normalized as Iran claims Hormuz closed. Qatar force majeure is unresolved. Jurong Island and Map Ta Phut feedstock pressure eases but does not resolve until Ras Laffan restarts. Source: SGX NJF1 futures (est.).

assets.name: Ethylene (CFR NE Asia)
assets.narrative: Ethylene follows naphtha lower on the ceasefire. SABIC and QAPCO export bottlenecks partially ease as ceasefire hopium enters the market. Agricultural film and irrigation pipe lead times remain stretched pending Hormuz normalization. Source: ICE/ICIS CFR NE Asia (est.).

assets.name: Methanol (CFR China)
assets.narrative: Methanol drops with the ceasefire relief as Iran - one of the world's largest methanol exporters - is part of the agreement. However, Qatar force majeure is still in effect and no formal export restart has been confirmed. China MTO complexes adjust to lower feedstock cost. Source: Investing.com CFR China (est.).

categories.label: Fertilizers
categories.supplyChainImpact: The Strait of Hormuz is the critical export artery for Middle East fertilizers: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman, and UAE together account for ~18% of global urea supply and ~12% of DAP. QatarEnergy (Qafco) - world's largest urea exporter - declared force majeure on Mar 2. Iran is also a significant potash and urea producer. Asian planting windows create an acute near-term demand cliff.

assets.name: Urea (FOB Middle East)
assets.narrative: Urea eases on ceasefire relief but remains structurally elevated: Qafco force majeure is still in effect, Iran claims Hormuz closed, and no confirmed export restart from Saudi or Qatar. Egypt and India continue buying at elevated premiums. Supply normalization requires formal Hormuz reopening + P&I restoration. Source: DTN, Argus Fertilizers (est.).

assets.name: DAP (FOB US Gulf)
assets.narrative: DAP eases as ceasefire reduces the tail risk of Saudi export dislocation. Saudi Ma'aden shipping remains disrupted; US Gulf supply continues filling the gap. Iran's East-West Pipeline attack on April 9 complicates Saudi infrastructure confidence. Wet-season planting pressure in Philippines and Vietnam persists through April.

assets.name: Potash (MOP CFR SE Asia)
assets.narrative: Potash freight costs ease with the ceasefire as war-risk premiums partially unwind. Less directly exposed to Hormuz than urea or DAP - freight normalization is the main transmission channel. Palm oil plantations in Malaysia and Indonesia benefit from lower input cost trajectory.

categories.label: Shipping & Freight
categories.supplyChainImpact: P&I club war risk coverage withdrawal (effective March 5) triggered mass tanker re-routing via Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 transit days and absorbing ~8% of global VLCC fleet capacity. Container lines have activated war risk surcharges and re-routed services, extending Asia-Europe transit times. Bunker fuel costs elevated by crude spike compound freight economics.

assets.name: Baltic Dry Index
assets.narrative: BDI sells off as ceasefire signals potential Hormuz normalization and Cape rerouting demand could reverse. However, Iran claims Hormuz still closed; vessels are not yet turning back from Cape routing. Normalization of dry-bulk flows depends on P&I restoration which requires formal Hormuz reopening. Source: Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News (est.).

assets.name: VLCC AG-Asia Rate
assets.narrative: VLCC rates crashed on ceasefire as some vessels began transiting Hormuz. However, Iran claims the strait is still closed (Lebanon strikes), and P&I insurance restoration requires a formal reopening + political settlement. The rate volatility reflects the ambiguity between the ceasefire announcement and Iran's actual Hormuz position. Source: Seatrade Maritime, Lloyd's List (est.).

assets.name: Container Freight (SCFI)
assets.narrative: Container rates ease on ceasefire hopium. Asia-Europe rates remain above pre-crisis levels as Suez normalization requires P&I restoration and formal Hormuz reopening - not yet confirmed. Southeast Asian manufacturers see some lead time improvement expectations. Source: Shanghai Shipping Exchange (est.).
